title: plsrri MATLAB Parity Audit (McIntosh Lab PLS)
text: Recent work R codebase focuses bringing method 3–6 scoring inference resampling closer MATLAB: MATLAB uses rri_get_behavscores() produce usc, vsc, lvcorrs. R now mirrors via pls_get_behavscores() uses pls_analysis() methods 3 & 5. MATLAB returns concatenated task + behavior score blocks stores per-block components (TBv, TBusc, TBvsc). R now computes stores fields methods 4 & 6, matching MATLAB’s row ordering block concatenation. MATLAB stacks v rows per group : [task rows] [behavior rows]. R’s pls_split_multiblock_v() now follows per-group interleaving instead assuming task rows precede behavior rows. (1–2): permute datamat via rri_perm_order (3–5): permute behavior rows (4–6): permute task block via rri_perm_order permute bscan rows via rri_randperm_notall R now follows logic uses Procrustes alignment (rri_bootprocrust) SVD-based methods keep LV correspondence. method 4 (rotated multiblock), R now applies SSQ rescaling step used MATLAB permutation significance testing. MATLAB computes bootstrap SEs scaled saliences (u * s) Procrustes alignment. R now computes compare_u (BSR) u_se using MATLAB‑style accumulation denominators, uses bscan-aware bootstrap orders multiblock.

what: Known legacy MATLAB edge-case bug (documented; not ported)
title: plsrri MATLAB Parity Audit (McIntosh Lab PLS)
text: vendored PLS/plscmd, missing-data helper stack (missnk_rri_xcor + misssum/missnk_mean) relies isnan(...) checks internally, Inf values consistently treated missing. Result: Inf inputs, MATLAB/Octave can propagate non-finite values correlation outputs. plsrri deliberately treats non-finite values (NA/NaN/Inf) missing pls_xcor() missing-data mode, preserving finite outputs data support exists. covered Octave parity tests: preserve MATLAB agreement NaN cases, intentionally porting Inf-propagation behavior. MATLAB/Octave computes perm_result.sprob sp / (num_perm + 1) regular permutation mode. plsrri uses sp / num_perm regular permutation tests (explicit number sampled permutations). treat MATLAB’s +1 normalization legacy behavior keep R implementation direct num_perm denominator. method 4 tiny trailing singular values, MATLAB R can disagree final LV count (sp) agreeing non-negligible LVs. Octave parity tests therefore assert strict equality stable LVs treat numerically null tail LV non-inferential.

title: plsrri MATLAB Parity Audit (McIntosh Lab PLS)
text: core non-GUI MATLAB parity gaps tracked addressed: Task brain-score CIs (stacked_smeanmat / usc2): implemented wired bootstrap CIs. Bootstrap CI distribution logic (rri_distrib.m): implemented (*_adj + prop). Split-half validation: updated match MATLAB’s outer-permutation / split distribution logic. SSB designs (ssb_*): num_subj_lst list support wired end--end analysis + perm + bootstrap. Missing-data variants (missnk_*): missing-aware cross-correlation implemented (missnk-style) used throughout. Remaining parity work now mostly GUI layer (PLS/plsgui/ vs inst/shiny/) adding “golden” MATLAB fixtures automated cross-language regression testing.

title: plsrri MATLAB Parity Audit (McIntosh Lab PLS)
text: goal faithful + idiomatic + fast R implementation: Implement stacked_smeanmat pls_get_covcor() port rri_distrib.m semantics fill CI fields already present pls_boot_result. Bring pls_splithalf_test() MATLAB parity (outer permutation + split-half correlation distributions). Wire ssb support end--end (allow num_subj_lst list--integers route ssb equivalents). Consolidate resampling loops use existing Rcpp helpers src/ (remove used). Export small MATLAB .mat fixture set compare R results via R.matlab available.

what: What data shape does Behavior PLS expect?
title: Behavior PLS Workflow
text: need two matrices stacked row structure (subjects within conditions, groups): Brain matrix — rows = observations, columns = voxels/features Behavior matrix — rows = observations, columns = behavioral measures example 18 subjects, 2 conditions, 120 voxels, two behavioral measures. synthetic data single latent factor driving RT accuracy opposite directions, plus condition-specific voxel block:

what: How do you run a behavior analysis?
title: Behavior PLS Workflow
text: behav_pls() convenience wrapper. runs Behavior PLS (method 3) behavior matrix second block: Check latent variables significant much variance explains: LV1 captures dominant brain-behavior relationship. scree plot makes structure clear: Singular value scree behavior PLS example. LV1 dominates.
code: behav_result <- behav_pls(   datamat_lst = list(brain_data),   behav_data = behav_data,   num_subj_lst = n_subj,   num_cond = n_cond,   nperm = 200,   nboot = 200,   progress = FALSE ) cbind(   pvalue = round(significance(behav_result), 3),   variance = round(singular_values(behav_result, normalize = TRUE), 1) ) #>     pvalue variance #> LV1      0     96.9 #> LV2      1      2.9 #> LV3      1      0.2 #> LV4      1      0.0 plot_singular_values(behav_result)

what: What data shape do we need?
title: Group by Covariate Designs
text: compact simulated example. Y_g1 Y_g2 subject--feature matrices. real analysis, columns voxels, parcels, surface vertices, another common feature space. trait matrix stacked group 1 followed group 2:
code: set.seed(42)  n1 <- 28 n2 <- 20 n_features <- 120  trait_g1 <- as.numeric(scale(rnorm(n1))) trait_g2 <- as.numeric(scale(rnorm(n2)))  Y_g1 <- matrix(rnorm(n1 * n_features, sd = 0.7), nrow = n1) Y_g2 <- matrix(rnorm(n2 * n_features, sd = 0.7), nrow = n2) head(trait, 4) #>           trait #> [1,]  0.9998323 #> [2,] -0.4992076 #> [3,]  0.2193336 #> [4,]  0.4282251 tail(trait, 4) #>           trait #> [45,] -1.177344 #> [46,]  0.656984 #> [47,] -0.610182 #> [48,]  1.686925

what: How do we test the trait effect and the interaction?
title: Group by Covariate Designs
text: Use non-rotated Behavior PLS hypothesis explicit. design matrix one row per group-specific trait association. first column asks shared trait-brain association; second asks whether association differs group. two requested contrasts become two latent variables: design loadings show hypothesis weights used latent variable: Interpret rows : common_trait: trait-brain association pooled across groups group_by_trait: difference trait-brain association groups sign group_by_trait follows contrast coding. c(-1, 1), positive brain saliences strongly associated trait group 2 group 1.
code: trait_design <- cbind(   common_trait = c(1, 1),   group_by_trait = c(-1, 1) )  trait_design #>      common_trait group_by_trait #> [1,]            1             -1 #> [2,]            1              1 trait_result <- pls_spec() |>   add_subjects(list(Y_g1, Y_g2), groups = c(n1, n2)) |>   add_conditions(1) |>   add_behavior(trait) |>   add_design(trait_design) |>   configure(     method = "behavior_nonrotated",     cormode = "pearson",     nperm = 199,     nboot = 199   ) |>   run(progress = FALSE) cbind(   pvalue = round(significance(trait_result), 3),   variance = round(singular_values(trait_result, normalize = TRUE), 1) ) #>     pvalue variance #> LV1      0     76.2 #> LV2      0     23.8 loadings(trait_result, type = "design") #>      contrast_1 contrast_2 #> [1,]  0.7071068 -0.7071068 #> [2,]  0.7071068  0.7071068

what: Can this be one multiblock analysis?
title: Group by Covariate Designs
text: Yes. Non-rotated Multiblock PLS combines task block behavior block. design, multiblock design rows : gives single analysis columns group main effect, common trait effect, group--trait effect. Use multiblock version want mean group difference trait associations represented one decomposition. Use separate non-rotated analyses want direct test term.
code: group 1 mean group 2 mean group 1 trait association group 2 trait association multiblock_design <- cbind(   group_2_minus_1 = c(-1, 1, 0, 0),   common_trait = c(0, 0, 1, 1),   group_by_trait = c(0, 0, -1, 1) )  multiblock_design #>      group_2_minus_1 common_trait group_by_trait #> [1,]              -1            0              0 #> [2,]               1            0              0 #> [3,]               0            1             -1 #> [4,]               0            1              1 multiblock_result <- pls_spec() |>   add_subjects(list(Y_g1, Y_g2), groups = c(n1, n2)) |>   add_conditions(1) |>   add_behavior(trait) |>   add_design(multiblock_design) |>   configure(     method = "multiblock_nonrotated",     cormode = "pearson",     nperm = 199,     nboot = 199   ) |>   run(progress = FALSE) cbind(   pvalue = round(significance(multiblock_result), 3),   variance = round(singular_values(multiblock_result, normalize = TRUE), 1) ) #>     pvalue variance #> LV1  0.261     35.8 #> LV2  0.869     24.4 #> LV3  0.131     39.8

what: How does Multiblock PLS differ from Behavior PLS?
title: Multiblock and Seed PLS Workflows
text: Behavior PLS, cross-block matrix captures brain-behavior covariance. Multiblock PLS, cross-block matrix built two stacked blocks: task block covering conditions (like Task PLS) behavior block covering selected subset conditions key decision block_conditions: conditions contribute behavior data. Typically choose conditions behavior meaningful (e.g., encoding retrieval, rest).

what: What does a multiblock analysis look like?
title: Multiblock and Seed PLS Workflows
text: example 14 subjects, 3 conditions (encoding, retrieval, rest), 140 voxels, two behavioral measures. synthetic data condition-driven signal (voxels 1-40), behavior-driven signal (voxels 41-80), interaction block (81-100). builder API natural way express multiblock analysis lets name conditions choose behavior-block conditions explicitly: result class pls_multiblock, indicating joint decomposition:
code: multiblock_result <- pls_spec() |>   add_subjects(list(brain_mb), groups = n_subj_mb) |>   add_conditions(3, labels = c("encoding", "retrieval", "rest")) |>   add_behavior(behav_mb, block_conditions = c("encoding", "retrieval")) |>   configure(method = "multiblock", nperm = 200, nboot = 200) |>   run(progress = FALSE) cbind(   pvalue = round(significance(multiblock_result), 3),   variance = round(singular_values(multiblock_result, normalize = TRUE), 1) ) #>     pvalue variance #> LV1   0.00     47.3 #> LV2   0.00     23.3 #> LV3   0.00     18.2 #> LV4   0.17      7.5 #> LV5   0.00      2.1 #> LV6   0.00      1.7 #> LV7   1.00      0.0

what: When is Seed PLS the simpler option?
title: Multiblock and Seed PLS Workflows
text: Seed PLS right choice secondary block already seed-region signal — mean activity ROI, seed time series connectivity analysis. Internally runs Behavior PLS (method 3) seed data behavior matrix, seed_pls() wrapper makes intent clear. example uses one seed region (posterior cingulate cortex) 16 subjects 2 conditions. Voxels 1-45 planted correlate seed; voxels 46-80 respond condition .
code: seed_result <- seed_pls(   datamat_lst = list(brain_seed),   seed_data = seed_data,   num_subj_lst = n_subj_seed,   num_cond = n_cond_seed,   nperm = 200,   nboot = 200,   progress = FALSE ) cbind(   pvalue = round(significance(seed_result), 3),   variance = round(singular_values(seed_result, normalize = TRUE), 1) ) #>     pvalue variance #> LV1  0.000     92.4 #> LV2  0.925      7.6

what: How does a PLS analysis work?
title: Getting Started with plsrri
text: core workflow three steps: Specify — describe data, conditions, groups, analysis parameters pls_spec object Run — execute analysis get pls_result containing singular values, saliences, scores, optional inference (permutation, bootstrap) Extract — pull need accessor functions: significance(), salience(), bsr(), scores(), loadings(), confidence() fastest path quick_pls(), wraps three steps one call. builder API (pls_spec() |> add_subjects() |> configure() |> run()) gives labels, metadata, full control every parameter.

what: What does a minimal analysis look like?
title: Getting Started with plsrri
text: vignette uses small synthetic dataset: two groups (young, older), three conditions, 200 voxels planted signal two blocks. group matrix 15 subjects ×\times 3 conditions = 45 rows 200 voxels: quick_pls() shortest path. runs mean-centering Task PLS permutation bootstrap inference one call: result object carries everything: 6 latent variables, p-value permutation test voxel-level bootstrap ratios.
code: rbind(   young = dim(group_young),   older = dim(group_older) ) #>       [,1] [,2] #> young   45  200 #> older   45  200 result <- quick_pls(   datamat_lst = list(group_young, group_older),   num_subj_lst = c(n_subj, n_subj),   num_cond = n_cond,   nperm = 200,   nboot = 200,   progress = FALSE )

Encoding (condition 1) loads positively; retrieval (condition 2) loads opposite direction. separation conditions confirms LV1 captures genuine experimental effect, noise.

title: Getting Started with plsrri
text: Brain scores show whether individual subjects express latent pattern. Tight distributions mean effect reliable; wide spread suggests individual differences. Brain scores LV1. point one subject-condition observation.

title: Getting Started with plsrri
text: Bootstrap ratios (BSRs) saliences divided bootstrap standard errors — work like z-scores. Voxels |BSR|>2|BSR| > 2 33 typically considered reliable. 21 200 voxels |BSR|>2|BSR| > 2. quick profile shows reliable voxels cluster: Bootstrap ratio profile across voxels. horizontal lines mark |BSR| = 2 threshold. Voxels 1-80 81-140 show planted signal blocks. can also threshold directly get cleaned vector:
code: lv1_bsr <- bsr(result, lv = 1) reliable <- sum(abs(lv1_bsr) > 2) lv1_bsr_clean <- bsr(result, lv = 1, threshold = 2) sum(lv1_bsr_clean != 0) #> [1] 21

Bootstrap intervals answer different question : specific brain-behavior links stable? four behavior--condition intervals stay away zero. supports claim symptom/reserve pattern stable, still held-predictive statement.

what: How do the inferential layers complement each other?
title: Predictive Measures and Resampling Inference
text: tools strongest treat complementary rather interchangeable: can read table four distinct questions: Decomposition permutation: latent variable stronger null PLS decomposition? Decomposition bootstrap: loadings correlations stable? Predictive regression/classification: learned score patterns generalize held-subjects? Split-half validation fits beside first two, last one. asks whether decomposition replicates across random halves data, evaluate_prediction() asks whether training-fit score space supports honest --fold prediction.

what: What does a pooled multisite dataset look like?
title: Multisite Behavioral PLS Workflow
text: pooled analysis still need standard behavior-PLS row structure: subjects within conditions. addition site label subject observation. example three sites, 36 pooled subjects, 2 conditions. hidden setup adds site-specific mean scale shifts standardization, pooled fit recover shared latent brain-behavior relation rather scanner/site offsets.
code: site_summary #>     site n_subjects n_observations       rt_mean      acc_mean #> 1 site_a         12             24 -5.898060e-17 -3.903128e-17 #> 2 site_b         12             24  3.670928e-17 -8.673617e-18 #> 3 site_c         12             24 -9.292516e-18  8.673617e-19

what: How do you fit one pooled model and keep site information?
title: Multisite Behavioral PLS Workflow
text: Use usual builder path behavioral PLS, attach site labels running fit. Check pooled latent variables first, just ordinary behavior PLS run: LV1 pooled brain-behavior pattern. important multisite question whether pooled pattern looks similar across sites.
code: site_spec <- pls_spec() |>   add_subjects(list(brain_z), groups = n_subj) |>   add_conditions(condition_levels) |>   add_behavior(behav_z) |>   add_site_labels(site_subj) |>   configure(method = "behavior", nperm = 120, nboot = 120)  site_result <- run(site_spec, progress = FALSE) site_diag <- site_result$site_diagnostics site_diag_infer <- site_pooling_diagnostics(   site_result,   spec = site_spec,   infer = "score",   nperm = 39,   nboot = 79,   progress = FALSE )  stopifnot(   inherits(site_result, "pls_result"),   is.list(site_diag),   is.list(site_diag_infer$score_heterogeneity),   setequal(site_diag$sites, site_levels) ) pooled_summary <- cbind(   pvalue = round(significance(site_result), 3),   variance = round(singular_values(site_result, normalize = TRUE), 1) )  stopifnot(   pooled_summary[1, "pvalue"] <= 0.1,   pooled_summary[1, "variance"] > pooled_summary[2, "variance"] )  pooled_summary #>     pvalue variance #> LV1  0.000     98.9 #> LV2  1.000      1.0 #> LV3  0.942      0.1 #> LV4  1.000      0.0

what: What diagnostics do you get when site labels are present?
title: Multisite Behavioral PLS Workflow
text: site_pooling_diagnostics() summarizes four things: sitewise score distributions sitewise brain-behavior score correlations site-specific refits compared pooled saliences leave-one-site-reruns site provided builder, diagnostics already attached result: Every site shows direction brain-behavior association LV1. first thing want see pooled multisite analysis. Sitewise LV1 brain-behavior score correlations. three sites show direction association.
code: site_corr_lv1 <- subset(site_diag$site_score_correlations, lv == 1)  stopifnot(   nrow(site_corr_lv1) == length(site_levels),   all(is.finite(site_corr_lv1$correlation)),   length(unique(sign(site_corr_lv1$correlation))) == 1L,   all(abs(site_corr_lv1$correlation) > 0.35) )  site_corr_lv1 #>     site lv n_obs n_subjects correlation #> 1 site_a  1    24         12   0.9610821 #> 2 site_b  1    24         12   0.9730672 #> 3 site_c  1    24         12   0.9562201 ggplot2::ggplot(site_corr_lv1, ggplot2::aes(x = site, y = correlation, fill = site)) +   ggplot2::geom_col(width = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +   ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "grey50") + ggplot2::labs(x = "Site", y = "LV1 score correlation") +   theme_pls()

what: Can you test whether those site correlations are too heterogeneous?
title: Multisite Behavioral PLS Workflow
text: Yes. infer = "score" adds subject-block bootstrap intervals site’s LV score correlation permutation p-value cross-site heterogeneity correlations. better answer “correlation fixed cutoff” asks whether site differences larger expect random subject--site reassignment pooled model. synthetic example, LV1 remains highly consistent across sites, heterogeneity p-value small. p-value small, pooled LV still significant overall failing multisite stability check.

what: What happens if you leave one site out?
title: Multisite Behavioral PLS Workflow
text: Leave-one-site-reruns strongest built-stress test current workflow. refit model two sites, project held-site score space. held-score correlations flip sign collapse toward zero, pooled latent pattern really shared. synthetic example held-site still lines training fit, want stable pooled analysis.

what: When should you stop pooling and rethink the model?
title: Multisite Behavioral PLS Workflow
text: Pooling still model choice. diagnostics meant tell pooled result real shared work papering site heterogeneity. Rethink pooled model see : small score-heterogeneity p-value pooled LV sitewise LV correlations different signs non-overlapping sitewise bootstrap intervals low site-specific salience similarity pooled LV, especially infer = "full" also shows weak subspace concordance leave-one-site-fits fail one particular site held score distributions dominated one site rather latent pattern point may want stronger harmonization, explicit site residualization, separate site-specific analyses.

what: Why not just use ordinary Seed PLS?
title: Within-Subject Seed PLS Workflow
text: seed_pls() works one seed value per subject-condition observation. useful want across-subject covariance seed activity rest brain. ws_seed_pls() asks different question: within given subject condition, seed voxel betas rise fall together trial trial? synthetic example built seed similar mean activation two conditions, trial--trial coupling voxels 1-15 flips sign: raison d’etre ws-seed workflow: connectivity differences can strong even seed’s average activation .
code: data.frame(   quantity = c(     "mean within-subject r in signal voxels (past)",     "mean within-subject r in signal voxels (future)",     "future - past difference in mean seed activation"   ),   value = round(c(signal_corr[["past"]], signal_corr[["future"]], seed_cond_diff), 2) ) #>                                           quantity value #> 1    mean within-subject r in signal voxels (past)  0.87 #> 2  mean within-subject r in signal voxels (future) -0.82 #> 3 future - past difference in mean seed activation  0.06

what: What does the basic ws-seed workflow look like?
title: Within-Subject Seed PLS Workflow
text: basic workflow : compute one seed--voxel connectivity map per subject per condition submit maps task PLS inspect significance, design scores, reliable voxels planted connectivity reversal strong, LV1 dominate: Singular values within-subject seed PLS example. LV1 captures dominant condition-dependent connectivity pattern. design scores show opposite contributions past future: Design scores LV1. two conditions pull opposite directions, reflecting planted sign flip within-subject connectivity.
code: ws_result <- ws_seed_pls(   beta_lst = beta_lst,   seed_lst = seed_lst,   condition_lst = condition_lst,   num_subj_lst = n_subj,   fisher_z = FALSE,   nperm = 200,   nboot = 200,   progress = FALSE )  stopifnot(   inherits(ws_result, "pls_result"),   inherits(ws_result, "pls_task"),   length(significance(ws_result)) >= 1L,   all(is.finite(ws_result$s)) ) cbind(   pvalue = round(significance(ws_result), 3),   variance = round(singular_values(ws_result, normalize = TRUE), 1) ) #>     pvalue variance #> LV1   0.00      100 #> LV2   0.51        0 plot_singular_values(ws_result) plot_scores(ws_result, lv = 1, type = "design", plot_type = "bar")

title: Within-Subject Seed PLS Workflow
text: 17 80 voxels pass |BSR|>2|BSR| > 2 threshold: Bootstrap ratio profile LV1. Voxels 1-15 carry planted within-subject connectivity effect.
code: ws_bsr <- bsr(ws_result, lv = 1) reliable <- sum(abs(ws_bsr) > 2)  stopifnot(   all(is.finite(ws_bsr)),   reliable > 10L )

title: Within-Subject Seed PLS Workflow
text: general multiseed layout seed_condition: rows = subject × seed × condition columns = voxels makes rotated non-rotated ws-seed analyses use underlying representation. row expansion makes targeted non-rotated design meaningful: can now test specific seed--condition hypotheses across explicit cells instead hiding seeds feature dimension.
code: data.frame(   condition_cell = multi_result$conditions,   design_row = seq_along(multi_result$conditions) ) #>     condition_cell design_row #> 1   Precuneus:past          1 #> 2 Precuneus:future          2 #> 3         IPS:past          3 #> 4       IPS:future          4

what: When would you use stacked_seed_features instead?
title: Within-Subject Seed PLS Workflow
text: stacked_seed_features keeps original task-PLS row structure: rows = subject × condition columns = voxel × seed can still useful, answers different question seed dimension now part feature space rather condition space. general recommendation : use seed_condition seed identity behave like experimental cell use stacked_seed_features explicitly want seed dimension folded feature space
code: stacked_result <- ws_seed_pls(   beta_lst = multi_beta_lst,   seed_lst = multi_seed_lst,   condition_lst = multi_condition_lst,   num_subj_lst = n_subj,   seed_labels = c("Precuneus", "IPS"),   layout = "stacked_seed_features",   fisher_z = FALSE,   nperm = 0,   nboot = 0,   progress = FALSE )  stopifnot(   stacked_result$num_cond == 2L,   identical(stacked_result$ws_seed_info$layout, "stacked_seed_features"),   identical(stacked_result$feature_layout$kind, "voxel_seed"),   nrow(stacked_result$u) == n_vox * 2L ) data.frame(   property = c("num_cond", "feature layout", "features in u"),   value = c(     stacked_result$num_cond,     stacked_result$feature_layout$kind,     nrow(stacked_result$u)   ) ) #>         property      value #> 1       num_cond          2 #> 2 feature layout voxel_seed #> 3  features in u        160

title: Run PLS Analysis — pls_analysis
text: Performs PLS (Partial Least Squares) analysis neuroimaging multivariate data. Supports task PLS, behavior PLS, multiblock PLS rotated non-rotated variants.

title: Run PLS Analysis — pls_analysis
code: pls_analysis(   datamat_lst,   num_subj_lst,   num_cond,   method = 1L,   num_perm = 0L,   num_boot = 0L,   num_split = 0L,   clim = 95,   stacked_behavdata = NULL,   stacked_designdata = NULL,   bscan = NULL,   meancentering_type = 0L,   cormode = 0L,   boot_type = "strat",   is_struct = FALSE,   permsamp = NULL,   Tpermsamp = NULL,   Bpermsamp = NULL,   bootsamp = NULL,   bootsamp_4beh = NULL,   parallel_config = NULL,   progress = TRUE )

title: Run PLS Analysis — pls_analysis
text: datamat_lst List data matrices. element matrix rows observations (subjects x conditions) columns features (voxels). list element represents one group. num_subj_lst Integer vector number subjects per group. num_cond Integer, number conditions. method Integer 1-6 specifying PLS method: 1 Mean-Centering Task PLS (default) 2 Non-Rotated Task PLS 3 Regular Behavior PLS 4 Regular Multiblock PLS 5 Non-Rotated Behavior PLS 6 Non-Rotated Multiblock PLS num_perm Number permutations (0 = permutation test). num_boot Number bootstrap samples (0 = bootstrap). num_split Number split-half validations (0 = split-half). clim Confidence level bootstrap (0-100, default 95). stacked_behavdata Behavior data matrix (required methods 3-6). Rows match stacked datamat, columns behavior measures. stacked_designdata Design contrast matrix (required methods 2, 5, 6). bscan Integer vector conditions behavior block multiblock PLS (methods 4, 6). Default conditions. meancentering_type Mean-centering type (0-3): 0 Remove within-group mean (default) 1 Remove grand condition mean 2 Remove grand mean 3 Remove main effects cormode Correlation mode behavior PLS (0, 2, 4, 6): 0 Pearson correlation (default) 2 Covariance 4 Cosine angle 6 Dot product boot_type Bootstrap type: "strat" (stratified, default) "nonstrat". is_struct Logical, TRUE structure PLS (permute conditions). permsamp Optional permutation reordering matrix (total_rows x num_perm). methods 3-6 treated behavior-block permutation order. Tpermsamp Optional task-block permutation matrix (total_rows x num_perm) multiblock methods (4, 6). Bpermsamp Optional behavior-block permutation matrix (total_rows x num_perm) multiblock/behavior methods. bootsamp Optional bootstrap reordering matrix (total_rows x num_boot). bootsamp_4beh Optional behavior bootstrap matrix (total_rows x num_boot) behavior/multiblock methods. parallel_config Optional parallel execution config list backend ("future" "sequential") workers. affects permutation testing task-bootstrap execution. progress Logical, show progress messages.

title: Run PLS Analysis — pls_analysis
text: pls_result object containing: method Integer, PLS method used u Salience matrix (voxels x LVs) s Singular values v Design/behavior loadings usc Brain scores vsc Design/behavior scores perm_result Permutation test results (num_perm > 0) boot_result Bootstrap results (num_boot > 0) splithalf_result Split-half results (num_split > 0)

title: Run PLS Analysis — pls_analysis
code: # Simple task PLS with 2 groups, 3 conditions set.seed(42) datamat1 <- matrix(rnorm(20 * 3 * 100), 60, 100)  # 20 subj x 3 cond datamat2 <- matrix(rnorm(15 * 3 * 100), 45, 100)  # 15 subj x 3 cond  result <- pls_analysis(   datamat_lst = list(datamat1, datamat2),   num_subj_lst = c(20, 15),   num_cond = 3,   method = 1 ) #> ℹ Stacking data matrices... #> ℹ Computing covariance/correlation matrix... #> ℹ Computing latent variables... #> ℹ Computing scores... #> ✔ PLS analysis complete

what: plsrri: Partial Least Squares for Neuroimaging — plsrri-package
title: plsrri: Partial Least Squares for Neuroimaging — plsrri-package
text: modern R implementation McIntosh Lab PLS package neuroimaging analysis. Supports task PLS, behavior PLS, multiblock PLS methods permutation testing, bootstrap confidence intervals, split-half validation. modern R implementation McIntosh Lab PLS package neuroimaging analysis. Supports task PLS, behavior PLS, multiblock PLS methods permutation testing, bootstrap confidence intervals, split-half validation. Integrates neuroim2 brain imaging data structures bidser BIDS directory access.

title: plsrri: Partial Least Squares for Neuroimaging — plsrri-package
text: package supports six PLS analysis methods: Method 1: Mean-Centering Task PLS Identifies patterns brain activity optimally distinguish experimental conditions. common approach task-based fMRI. Method 2: Non-Rotated Task PLS Tests specific hypotheses condition differences using predefined contrasts. Use specific predictions. Method 3: Regular Behavior PLS Identifies brain patterns maximally correlate behavioral measures. brain-behavior relationship analysis. Method 4: Regular Multiblock PLS Combines task effects behavior-brain correlations single analysis. Examines task behavior simultaneously. Method 5: Non-Rotated Behavior PLS Tests specific hypotheses behavior-brain relationships using predefined contrasts. Method 6: Non-Rotated Multiblock PLS Tests combined hypotheses task effects behavior-brain relationships.

title: plsrri: Partial Least Squares for Neuroimaging — plsrri-package
text: recommended way run analyses using builder API:
code: result <- pls_spec(mask = brain_mask) |>   add_subjects(bids_dir, groups = c("control", "patient")) |>   add_conditions(events_df) |>   configure(method = "task", nperm = 1000, nboot = 500) |>   run()

title: plsrri: Partial Least Squares for Neuroimaging — plsrri-package
text: Analysis pls_analysis: Core analysis function pls_spec: Create analysis specification Builder API add_subjects: Add subject data add_conditions: Specify conditions add_behavior: Add behavioral measures add_design: Add design contrasts configure: Set analysis parameters run: Execute analysis Results salience: Extract brain loadings bsr: Extract bootstrap ratios scores: Extract brain/design scores significance: Get permutation p-values confidence: Get bootstrap CIs Visualization plot_brain: Brain slice plots plot_scores: Score plots plot_loadings: Loading bar plots render_report: Generate Quarto report

title: plsrri: Partial Least Squares for Neuroimaging — plsrri-package
text: package integrates : neuroim2: Imaging data structures (NeuroVol, NeuroVec) bidser: BIDS directory access fMRIPrep derivatives neurosurf: Surface visualization (planned)

title: plsrri: Partial Least Squares for Neuroimaging — plsrri-package
text: large-scale analyses (500+ subjects, 10k permutations), package provides Rcpp-accelerated versions key functions. default exact fast paths tuned conservatively: "xcor": enabled default "bootstrap": enabled default "permutation": available, disabled default Use options(plsrri.fast_paths = c("xcor", "bootstrap")) make default explicit, options(plsrri.fast_paths = c("xcor", "bootstrap", "permutation")) opt permutation fast paths, options(plsrri.fast_paths = FALSE) disable exact fast paths entirely.

title: Within-Subject Seed PLS (ws-fcMRI) — ws-seed-pls
text: Functions within-subject seed-based PLS analysis, implementing ws-seed PLS method described Roberts et al. (2016). Unlike standard seed PLS (-fcMRI), correlates seed voxel activity across subjects, ws-seed PLS correlates seed voxel trial-level beta estimates within subject, submits resulting connectivity maps task PLS.

title: Within-Subject Seed PLS (ws-fcMRI) — ws-seed-pls
text: approach requires trial-level activation estimates (e.g., fmrilss::lss()) rather condition means. subject condition, Pearson correlations computed seed region's trial--trial betas every voxel's trial--trial betas. resulting within-subject correlation maps (optionally Fisher-z transformed) stacked data matrix analyzed task PLS.

title: Within-Subject Seed PLS (ws-fcMRI) — ws-seed-pls
text: Roberts, R. P., Hach, S., Tippett, L. J., & Addis, D. R. (2016). Simpson's paradox fMRI: Similarities differences functional connectivity measures derived within-subject across-subject correlations. NeuroImage. doi:10.1016/j.neuroimage.2016.04.028

title: Compute Within-Subject Seed-Voxel Correlation Maps — ws_seed_correlation
text: beta_lst list matrices (one per subject). matrix rows = trials, columns = voxels. matrices must number columns (voxels). Trial counts may vary across subjects. seed_lst list numeric vectors single-column matrices (one per subject). element contains seed region's trial-level betas, length equal number trials (rows) subject. matrix multiple columns provided, column treated separate seed correlations computed independently . condition_lst list factors integer vectors (one per subject). element maps trials conditions (length rows corresponding beta matrix). Condition labels must consistent across subjects. fisher_z Logical; TRUE (default), apply Fisher's r--z transform within-subject correlations stacking. min_trials Integer; minimum number trials per condition required compute valid correlation (default 3). Conditions fewer trials produce NA values.

title: Compute Within-Subject Seed-Voxel Correlation Maps — ws_seed_correlation
text: list components: datamat matrix rows = subjects x conditions (stacked condition-within-subject order, .e., conditions subject 1, conditions subject 2, etc.) columns = voxels (x n_seeds multiple seeds). multiple seeds, columns ordered voxels seed 1, voxels seed 2, etc. num_cond Integer; number conditions. cond_labels Character vector condition labels. n_subjects Integer; number subjects. n_seeds Integer; number seed regions. n_voxels Integer; number voxels per seed.

title: Compute Within-Subject Seed-Voxel Correlation Maps — ws_seed_correlation
text: stacking order places conditions within subjects (matching convention used pls_analysis()):
code: row 1:  subject 1, condition 1   row 2:  subject 1, condition 2   ...   row k:  subject 1, condition k   row k+1: subject 2, condition 1   ...

title: Compute Within-Subject Seed-Voxel Correlation Maps — ws_seed_correlation
code: # Simulate trial-level data for 10 subjects, 2 conditions, 50 voxels set.seed(42) n_subj <- 10; n_trials <- 24; n_vox <- 50; n_cond <- 2 beta_lst <- lapply(seq_len(n_subj), function(i) matrix(rnorm(n_trials * n_vox), n_trials, n_vox)) seed_lst <- lapply(seq_len(n_subj), function(i) rnorm(n_trials)) cond_lst <- lapply(seq_len(n_subj), function(i) rep(1:n_cond, each = n_trials / n_cond))  ws <- ws_seed_correlation(beta_lst, seed_lst, cond_lst) dim(ws$datamat)  # 20 x 50 #> [1] 20 50

title: Within-Subject Seed PLS Analysis — ws_seed_pls
text: Convenience function within-subject seed-based PLS (ws-seed PLS). Computes within-subject seed-voxel correlations trial-level beta estimates, runs task PLS resulting connectivity maps. implements ws-seed PLS method Roberts et al. (2016), captures temporal co-fluctuation seed target regions within individual, avoiding Simpson's Paradox can arise standard across-subject seed PLS.

title: Within-Subject Seed PLS Analysis — ws_seed_pls
code: ws_seed_pls(   beta_lst,   seed_lst,   condition_lst,   num_subj_lst,   groups = NULL,   seed_labels = NULL,   layout = c("seed_condition", "stacked_seed_features"),   fisher_z = TRUE,   min_trials = 3L,   nonrotated = FALSE,   meancentering_type = 0L,   nperm = 1000,   nboot = 500,   nsplit = 0,   clim = 95,   boot_type = "strat",   is_struct = FALSE,   progress = TRUE,   stacked_designdata = NULL,   ... )

title: Within-Subject Seed PLS Analysis — ws_seed_pls
text: beta_lst list matrices (one per subject). matrix rows = trials, columns = voxels. Typically produced fmrilss::lss(). seed_lst list numeric vectors matrices (one per subject). contains seed region's trial-level betas. matrix, column separate seed. condition_lst list factors integer vectors (one per subject) mapping trials experimental conditions. num_subj_lst Integer vector number subjects per group. single group, just total number subjects. multiple groups, vector (e.g., c(12, 14)). SSB designs, supply list integer vectors giving subject counts per condition within group. groups Optional subject-level group assignments trial inputs. Required num_subj_lst uses SSB/list form subject-group membership recovered per-condition counts alone. seed_labels Optional character vector naming supplied seeds. layout Layout used build ws-seed data matrix. Use "seed_condition" (default) one row per seed-condition cell, "stacked_seed_features" keep rows condition level stack seed-specific voxel maps feature axis. fisher_z Logical; apply Fisher r--z transform (default TRUE). min_trials Minimum trials per condition valid correlation (default 3). nonrotated Logical; TRUE, use non-rotated task PLS (method 2). Default FALSE uses standard rotated task PLS (method 1). meancentering_type Mean-centering task PLS: 0 Within-group centering (default) 1 Grand condition mean removal 2 Grand mean removal 3 main effects removal nperm Number permutations (default 1000). nboot Number bootstrap samples (default 500). nsplit Number split-half resamples. clim Confidence level bootstrap confidence intervals. boot_type Bootstrap strategy passed run(). is_struct Logical; request structure coefficients supported. progress Logical; show progress output running analysis. stacked_designdata Optional explicit design/contrast matrix non-rotated analyses. ... Additional arguments passed pls_analysis().

title: Within-Subject Seed PLS Analysis — ws_seed_pls

title: Within-Subject Seed PLS Analysis — ws_seed_pls
text: analysis proceeds two stages: Within-subject correlation: subject condition, Pearson correlations computed seed's trial-level betas every voxel's trial-level betas. produces one connectivity map per subject per condition. Task PLS: connectivity maps stacked submitted task PLS, identifies latent variables capturing condition-dependent connectivity patterns reliable across subjects. Trial-level betas typically estimated using Least Squares Separate (LSS) via fmrilss::lss(), provides stable per-trial activation estimates even rapid event-related designs.

title: Within-Subject Seed PLS Analysis — ws_seed_pls

title: plsrri 0.1.0
text: Added deterministic Octave-backed parity tests core MATLAB PLS/plscmd helpers non-rotated end--end analysis methods (2, 5, 6). Added deterministic method-4 Octave parity coverage using fixed resampling matrices (permsamp/Tpermsamp bootsamp/bootsamp_4beh). Added deterministic methods-3/5 Octave parity coverage fixed permsamp/bootsamp paths, including permutation/bootstrap output checks. Added deterministic method-1 Octave parity coverage fixed permsamp/bootsamp paths, including inference-output checks. Added deterministic methods-2/6 Octave parity coverage fixed permutation/bootstrap order matrices, including inference-output checks. Added support caller-supplied permutation/bootstrap order matrices pls_analysis() wired pass-inference internals. Added generated Octave split-half fixture coverage tests/testthat/fixtures/matlab/splithalf_result.rds. Documented intentional correctness divergence missing-data handling: plsrri treats non-finite values (NA/NaN/Inf) missing pls_xcor() missing-data mode, legacy MATLAB missnk_* utilities can propagate non-finite output Inf inputs.
